_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
**1. Fuel-cost monitoring and surcharge-tracking infrastructure**
Because the SAF Mandate's compliance costs (SAF blending premium or buy-out at £4.70/litre main / £5.00/litre PtL) will be passed through airline and fuel-supplier pricing into air-freight rates on UK lanes, the workspace must build or enhance a **carrier surcharge monitoring system** capable of disaggregating SAF-related cost components from standard fuel surcharges. Scope: all UK-origin and UK-destination air-freight lanes (Americas–UK, Europe–UK, Asia–UK). Baseline gap: most freight forwarder TMS/rate-management tools do not yet itemise SAF surcharges separately from fuel surcharges. Target state: real-time visibility of per-kg SAF cost contribution by airline and route. No statutory deadline — driven by commercial necessity as SAF mandates escalate annually.

**2. Scope 3 / client sustainability data collection**
The workspace's clients in live events, fine art, luxury, film/TV, and high-value automotive verticals are increasingly subject to their own sustainability reporting obligations (e.g., UK TCFD, CSRD for European subsidiaries). SAF certificate data — specifically the GHG reduction percentage achieved by the SAF blended into fuel on a given shipment — must be requested from airline partners and incorporated into per-shipment emissions reporting. Scope: all air shipments on UK-routed lanes. The workspace should build a **data-collection process** to request SAF blend percentage and certificate reference numbers from airline partners, aligned with HMRC-verified data published by DfT under the SAF Mandate Statistics series. Gap from baseline: no standardised airline-to-forwarder SAF data transmission protocol currently exists; voluntary engagement with airline sustainability teams is the current pathway.

**3. Carrier and fuel-supplier contract clause review**
Existing carrier service agreements and fuel-procurement-adjacent contracts should be reviewed to confirm: (a) there is no clause that inadvertently positions the workspace as a fuel-supply intermediary (which could trigger SAF Mandate obligations); (b) SAF surcharge pass-through mechanisms are clearly defined and capped; (c) provisions exist for the workspace to receive C&S data from the carrier for client sustainability reporting. Legal review recommended, particularly for bespoke charter arrangements used in live events and film/TV verticals. Deadline: before renewal of any UK air-freight master service agreements.

**4. Third-party assurance awareness (not a direct workspace obligation)**
The SAF Mandate requires fuel suppliers to engage Administrator-approved third-party verifiers for all certificate applications; carbon and sustainability data must be independently verified before certificates are awarded. While the workspace has no direct assurance obligation, it should confirm — as part of supplier qualification — that its airline and fuel-supplier partners have engaged approved verifiers. The DfT publishes a list of approved verifiers and voluntary schemes. This is a due-diligence step rather than a system build, but should be embedded in the workspace's supplier onboarding checklist. No formal deadline, but relevant for each obligation year.

**5. Revenue Certainty Mechanism (RCM) monitoring**
The SAF Bill, progressing through Parliament (Report Stage, House of Commons, as of mid-2025), is expected to become law by end of 2026. The resulting Guaranteed Strike Price mechanism will establish long-term SAF pricing contracts between a government-owned counterparty and UK SAF producers. This will materially affect the long-term trajectory of SAF premiums. The workspace should assign a regulatory monitoring owner to track RCM secondary legislation development and update its long-term air-freight cost models annually.

*Source: SAF Mandate Compliance Guidance 2025, DfT, Jan 2025. https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/6762717b26a2d1ff182534f7/saf-mandate-compliance-guidance-2025.pdf; RTFO and SAF Mandate Third Party Assurance, DfT, Dec 2025. https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/rtfo-and-saf-mandate-third-party-assurance; Addleshaw Goddard LLP, May 2025. https://www.addleshawgoddard.com/en/insights/insights-briefings/2025/energy/uks-sustainable-aviation-fuel-bill-revenue-certainty-mechanism/*
